texts.orig: Identification by non-Gaussianity in structural threshold and smooth transition vector autoregressive models
texts.text: Identification by non-Gaussianity in structural threshold and smooth transition vector autoregressive models

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Linear structural vector autoregressive models can be identified statistically without imposing restrictions on the model if the shocks are mutually independent and at most one of them is Gaussian. We show that this result extends to structural threshold and smooth transition vector autoregressive models incorporating a time-varying impact matrix defined as a weighted sum of the impact matrices of the regimes. We also discuss the problem of labelling the shocks, estimation of the parameters, and stationarity the model. The introduced methods are implemented to the accompanying R package sstvars. Our empirical application studies the effects of the climate policy uncertainty shock on the U.S. macroeconomy. In a structural logistic smooth transition vector autoregressive model with two regimes, we find that a positive climate policy uncertainty shock decreases production and increases inflation in times of both low and high economic policy uncertainty, but its inflationary effects are stronger in the periods of high economic policy uncertainty.
texts.text: Linear structural vector autoregressive models can be identified statistically without imposing restrictions on the model if the shocks are mutually independent and at most one of them is Gaussian. We show that this result extends to structural threshold and smooth transition vector autoregressive models incorporating a time-varying impact matrix defined as a weighted sum of the impact matrices of the regimes. We also discuss the problem of labelling the shocks, estimation of the parameters, and stationarity the model. The introduced methods are implemented to the accompanying R package sstvars. Our empirical application studies the effects of the climate policy uncertainty shock on the U.S. macroeconomy. In a structural logistic smooth transition vector autoregressive model with two regimes, we find that a positive climate policy uncertainty shock decreases production and increases inflation in times of both low and high economic policy uncertainty, but its inflationary effects are stronger in the periods of high economic policy uncertainty.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1 Introduction
texts.text: 1 Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Linear vector autoregressive (VAR) models are a standard tool in empirical macroeconomics due to their ability to effectively capture constant dynamic relationships of the included variables. Macroeconomic systems may, however, exhibit variation in their dynamics, induced by wars, crises, policy shifts, or business cycle fluctuations, for example. Such structural changes cannot be accommodated by linear VAR models, and therefore, nonlinear VAR models that are able capture such dynamics are often employed. Nonlinear structural VAR (SVAR) models are particularly useful, as they facilitate tracing out the causal effects of economic shocks, which may vary in time depending on the initial state of the economy as well as on the sign and size of the shock (Kilian and L¨ utkepohl, 2017, Chapter 18). To estimate these effects, the economic shocks need to be identified. Conventional identification methods typically employed in nonlinear SV AR models rely on restrictive assumptions, such as zero contemporaneous interactions among some of the variables (see Kilian and L¨ utkepohl, 2017, Section 18.2.2). Such restrictions with a clear interpretation are advantageous when based on economic reasoning, but often they are economically implausible and imposed just to achieve sufficient identification. To overcome the latter issue, statistical identification methods relying on the statistical properties of the data can be used.

texts.orig: There are two main branches in the statistical identification literature: identification by heteroscedasticity (Rigobon, 2003, Lanne, L¨ utkepohl, and Maciejowska, 2010, Bacchiocchi and Fanelli, 2015, L¨ utkepohl and Netˇ sunajev, 2017, Lewis, 2021, Virolainen, 2025b, and others) and identification by non-Gaussianity (Lanne, Meitz, and Saikkonen, 2017, Lanne and Luoto, 2021, Lanne, Liu, and Luoto, 2023, and others). To the best our knowledge, this paper is the first to study identification by non-Gaussianity in nonlinear SVAR models. Under certain statistical conditions, both types of identification methods typically identify the shocks of a linear structural vector autoregressive (SVAR) model without imposing further restrictions. texts.label: text
texts.orig: This paper contributes to the literature on identification by non-Gaussianity by extending the framework proposed by Lanne et al. (2017) to smooth transition vector autoregressive (STVAR) models (e.g., Anderson and Vahid, 1998), which is major class of nonlinear SVAR models. We also consider the threshold vector autoregressive model of Tsay (1998) as a special case of the STVAR model. The STVAR model can flexibly capture nonlinear data generating dynamics by accommodating multiple regimes and gradual shifts between them, governed by the transition weighs. In contrast to its linear counterpart, the impact matrix of the structural STV AR model should generally allow for time-variation in the impact responses of the variables to the shocks, which complicates identification. Nonetheless, similarly to Lanne et al. (2017), it turns out that identification is achieved when the shocks are mutually independent and at most one of them is Gaussian. We show that under this condition, when the impact matrix of the STVAR model is defined as a weighted sum of the impact matrices of the regimes, the shocks are readily identified up to ordering and signs. The weights of the impact matrices are the transition weights of the regimes, which we assume to be either exogenous, logistic, or of the threshold form, but our results can be extended to other suitable weight functions as well.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In line with the statistical identification literature, external information is required to label the identified structural shocks as economic shocks. Our nonlinear setup has the additional complication that the same shock should be assigned to the same column of the impact matrix across all regimes. Therefore, since our experience shows that the identification can be weak with respect to the ordering and signs of the columns of the regime-specific impact matrices, we recommend adopting a blended identification strategy that combines identification by non-Gaussianity with additional identifying information (cf. Carriero, Marcellino, and Tornese, 2024).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: called independent innovation analysis (IIA). The IIA estimates the (conditionally) contemporaneously independent innovations of a nonlinear SVAR model without imposing any specific functional form. Under fairly general assumptions, their method consistently estimates the independent innovations up to permutation and scalar component-wise invertible transformations. While the IIA does seem promising, it is not obvious how to apply it in empirical macroeconometrics, as identification only up to such transformations is, in our understanding, insufficient for structural analysis. Moreover, Morioka et al. (2021) assume the innovations follow a distribution in the exponential family, thereby excluding various interesting alternatives. In contrast, our approach facilitates estimation of the parameters of an identified nonlinear SV AR model, enabling structural analysis without restricting the distribution of independent shocks beyond ruling out Gaussian and infinite-variance cases.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our empirical application studies the macroeconomic effects of climate policy uncertainty (shocks) and considers monthly U.S. data from 1987:4 to 2024:12. Following Khalil and Strobel (2023) and Huang and Punzi (2024), we measure climate policy uncertainty (CPU) with the CPU index (Gavriilidis, 2021), which is constructed based on the amount of newspaper coverage on topics related to CPU. We are interested in studying how the effects of the CPU shock vary depending on the level of economic policy uncertainty (EPU). Therefore, we fit a two-regime structural logistic STVAR model using the first lag of the EPU index (Baker, Bloom, and Davis, 2016) as the switching variable. We find that a positive CPU shock decreases production and increases inflation in times of both low and high EPU, but its inflationary effects are stronger in the periods of high EPU. Our results are, hence, in line with the previous literature suggesting that a positive CPU shock decreases production (Fried, Novan, and Peterman, 2022, Khalil and Strobel, 2023, and Huang and Punzi, 2024) and increases inflation (Khalil and Strobel, 2023, and Huang and Punzi, 2024).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The result of Lemma 1 holds almost everywhere in [ B 1 : ... : B M ] ∈ R d × dM , which means that the set where the matrices B 1 , ..., B M are such that B y,t is singular for some t has a Lebesgue measure zero. This implies that invertible matrices B 1 , ..., B M generally lead to an invertible impact matrix B y,t , excluding some special cases of B 1 , ..., B M for which this result does not hold (e.g., if B 2 = -B 1 , then B y,t = 0 when α 1 ,t = α 2 ,t = 0 . 5 ). To further improve intuition, note that the result of Lemma 1 implies that if the matrices B 1 , ..., B M are drawn by random from a continuous distribution, the probability of obtaining matrices such that B y,t is singular for some t is zero. In practice, the parameter space should be simply restricted in estimation so that parameter values leading to B y,t that is singular for some t are excluded.

texts.orig: If the impact matrix is time-invariant as in Lanne et al. (2017), i.e., B y,t = B for some constant matrix B , it follows from Lemma 2 that the structural shocks are identified up to ordering and signs. Such statistically identified shocks can then often be labelled as economic shocks based on external information (see Lanne et al. , 2017). However, when the impact matrix varies in time, two complications arise. First, as the impact matrix B y,t is not a matrix of constant parameters but a function of parameters, it needs to be shown that the parameters in the specific functional form of B y,t are identified. Second, since the identification of B y,t at each t is only up to ordering and signs of its columns, its unique identification requires that the parameters in the functional form of B y,t are restricted to fix the ordering and signs of the columns of B y,t .

By Lemma 2, B y,t is thereby identified up to ordering and signs of its columns within each regime, and identification requires constraints that fix these orderings and signs for each regime-specific impact matrix B 1 , ..., B M . For simplicity, and to state the identification result under just-identifying constraints, we assume each shock e it , i = 1 , ..., d , follows the skewed t -distribution of Hansen (1994), which can flexibly capture fat tails and skewness, and nests the Gaussian distribution as a special case. texts.orig: Having established the identification result for structural TV AR models, we now consider transition weights that exhibit more variation. In particular, the transition weights are here assumed to be either exogenous (nonrandom) or logistic as defined in (2.4), so that the result of Lemma 2 establishing identification of the transition weights can be exploited. Under the conditions of the following proposition (which is proven in Appendix B.4), the matrices B 1 , ..., B M are uniquely identified.

If the shocks follow the skewed t -distributions defined in (4.1), any fixed ordering and signs of the columns of B 1 can be assumed without loss of generality, as reordering the columns would just reorder the shocks and swapping a sign of a column corresponds to swapping the sign of the related shock and skewness parameter. Condition (B) states that the transition weights should, for some t , be strictly positive for all of the regimes. If there are only two regimes, the requirement is merely that the regime-switches are not always discrete, in which case Proposition 1 would be relevant. texts.label: text
texts.orig: As in the linear SV AR model of Lanne et al. (2017), the statistically identified structural shocks do not necessarily have an economic interpretation, and labelling them as economic shocks requires external information. Our identification method, nonetheless, facilitates identifying structural shocks with time-varying impact effects without imposing restrictions on the impact responses of the variables. This is a major advantage compared to statistical identification by heteroscedasticity that restricts how the impact responses may vary over time (see L¨ utkepohl and Netˇ sunajev, 2017).

texts.orig: To formally obtain the local solution that facilitates labelling the shock of interest to the same column of the impact matrix in all regimes, we recommend employing a 'blended identification' strategy that combines identification by non-Gaussianity with additional identifying information (cf. Carriero et al. , 2024). As a concrete example, if a supply shock should increase output and decrease prices at impact in all regimes, selecting the best local solution where the same column in all B 1 , ..., B M satisfies such signs amounts to combining identification by non-Gaussianity with the appropriate short-run sign restrictions. In addition to short-run sign restrictions, also other types of information can be exploited in blended identification, including narrative restrictions and testable zero impact effect restrictions, for instance.

texts.orig: over the parameter space. To obtain a well-behaving estimator with desirable asymptotic properties such as consistency, the parameter space is often restricted to the region where the model is ergodic stationary. Based on the results of Saikkonen (2008) (see also Kheifets and Saikkonen, 2020), we show in Appendix A (Theorem A.1) that when the transition weights α m,t are F t -1 -measurable functions of { y t -j , j = 1 , ..., p } , a sufficient condition for ergodic stationarity is that the joint spectral radius of the companion form AR matrices of the regimes is strictly less than one. But since this condition is computationally demanding to verify (e.g., Chang and Blondel, 2013), it is not very practical to use it to restrict the parameter space in estimation. Therefore, we restrict the parameter space with the more easily verifiable condition that the usual stability condition is satisfied for each of the regimes (Condition A.1 in Appendix A), as it is necessary for the sufficient condition. After estimation, the sufficient condition can be checked for the solutions of interest.

To obtain a well-behaving estimator with desirable asymptotic properties such as consistency, the parameter space is often restricted to the region where the model is ergodic stationary. Based on the results of Saikkonen (2008) (see also Kheifets and Saikkonen, 2020), we show in Appendix A (Theorem A.1) that when the transition weights α m,t are F t -1 -measurable functions of { y t -j , j = 1 , ..., p } , a sufficient condition for ergodic stationarity is that the joint spectral radius of the companion form AR matrices of the regimes is strictly less than one. But since this condition is computationally demanding to verify (e.g., Chang and Blondel, 2013), it is not very practical to use it to restrict the parameter space in estimation. Therefore, we restrict the parameter space with the more easily verifiable condition that the usual stability condition is satisfied for each of the regimes (Condition A.1 in Appendix A), as it is necessary for the sufficient condition. After estimation, the sufficient condition can be checked for the solutions of interest.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Maximizing the log-likelihood function (presented in Section 4.2) can be challenging in practice due to its high multimodality, induced by the nonlinear dynamics. Imposing the stability condition can make estimation particularly difficult when the data is persistent, as in such cases numerical optimization algorithms frequently gravitate to the boundary of the parameter space, where they perform poorly and are not often even able to converge to a local maximum. To overcome this issue, we propose to allow for unstable estimates and to maximize the penalized log-likelihood function, obtained by adding a penalty term to the log-likelihood function that penalizes parameter values falling outside or close to the boundary of the stability region. In this way, the optimization algorithm can explore the parameter space also outside the stability region, facilitating improved performance, while sufficient penalization eventually steers the optimization algorithm back to the stability region.

The nonlinear dynamics of our structural STVAR model, however, add substantial challenges to the estimation problem by inducing a high number of modes to the (penalized) log-likelihood function. The nonlinear dynamics of our structural STVAR model, however, add substantial challenges to the estimation problem by inducing a high number of modes to the (penalized) log-likelihood function. To address these challenges, we propose a modified version of the three-step estimation procedure in which multimodality is explicitly taken into account.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our three-step estimation procedure proceeds with the following steps:
texts.text: Our three-step estimation procedure proceeds with the following steps:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Estimate the autoregressive and weight function parameters θ 1 ≡ ( ϕ 1 , ..., ϕ M , φ 1 , ..., φ M , α ) by the method of (penalized) nonlinear least squares (NLS) (see Appendix C for details). Denote this estimate of θ 1 as ˆ θ NLS 1 .
texts.text: Estimate the autoregressive and weight function parameters θ 1 ≡ ( ϕ 1 , ..., ϕ M , φ 1 , ..., φ M , α ) by the method of (penalized) nonlinear least squares (NLS) (see Appendix C for details). Denote this estimate of θ 1 as ˆ θ NLS 1 .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. Estimate the error distribution parameters θ 2 ≡ ( σ, ν ) by (penalized) ML with a genetic algorithm conditionally on the (penalized) NLS estimate ˆ θ NLS 1 . Denote the obtained estimate of θ 2 as ˆ θ GA 2 .
texts.text: Estimate the error distribution parameters θ 2 ≡ ( σ, ν ) by (penalized) ML with a genetic algorithm conditionally on the (penalized) NLS estimate ˆ θ NLS 1 . Denote the obtained estimate of θ 2 as ˆ θ GA 2 .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. Estimate the full parameter vector θ = ( θ 1 , θ 2 ) by (penalized) ML by initializing a gradient based optimization algorithm from ( ˆ θ NLS 1 , ˆ θ GA 2 ) .
texts.text: Estimate the full parameter vector θ = ( θ 1 , θ 2 ) by (penalized) ML by initializing a gradient based optimization algorithm from ( ˆ θ NLS 1 , ˆ θ GA 2 ) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Step 1 obtains initial estimates for the AR and weight function parameters, and it is comparable to Step 1 of Lanne et al. (2017) but with the nonlinearity of the model explicitly accounted for. Notably, the NLS estimation often produces estimates that do not satisfy the usual stability condition for each of the regimes, and thus allowing for instability (but penalizing it) facilitates utilization of the NLS estimates in our three-step procedure. Step 2 is comparable to Step 2 of Lanne et al. (2017), but the multimodality of the (penalized) log-likelihood function is taken into account by making use of a robust estimation algorithm that is able to escape from local maxima. Step 3 finalizes the estimation by initializing a standard gradient based optimization algorithm from the initial estimates obtained from the previous steps, often converging to a nearby local maximum of the (penalized) log-likelihood function.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Due to the high multimodality of the (penalized) log-likelihood function, Steps 2 and 3 should be ran a large number times to improve the reliability of the results. Since multiple estimation rounds are run, the estimation procedure produces a set of estimates, presumably corresponding to different local maximums of the (penalized) log-likelihood function. If there are multiple solutions with (penalized) log-likelihoods almost equal to the greatest found (penalized) log-likelihood, the blended identification procedure discussed in Section 3.5 can be employed to obtain the best local solution that facilitates labelling the shocks of interest. The proposed three-step estimation procedure is implemented in the accompanying R package sstvars (Virolainen, 2025a), which is available via the CRAN repository.

texts.orig: To assess the performance of the penalized maximum likelihood (PML) estimator as well as our three-step estimation procedure, we conduct a small-scale Monte Carlo study, which is discussed in detail in Appendix D. Since estimation is computationally demanding, we consider simple bivariate (structural) TV AR and LSTVAR models with p = 1 and M = 2 . To evaluate how the PML estimator performs when the AR matrices are in the penalization region, we consider two specifications of both TV AR and LSTVAR models: one where the AR matrices are well inside the stability region and one where they lie clearly in the region where the penalization term is strictly positive.
texts.text: To assess the performance of the penalized maximum likelihood (PML) estimator as well as our three-step estimation procedure, we conduct a small-scale Monte Carlo study, which is discussed in detail in Appendix D. Since estimation is computationally demanding, we consider simple bivariate (structural) TV AR and LSTVAR models with p = 1 and M = 2 . To evaluate how the PML estimator performs when the AR matrices are in the penalization region, we consider two specifications of both TV AR and LSTVAR models: one where the AR matrices are well inside the stability region and one where they lie clearly in the region where the penalization term is strictly positive.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: According to the results (see Tables D.2 and D.3 in Appendix D for details), estimation accuracy is slightly better when the AR matrices are inside the stability region, but even when they are in the penalization region, the estimators of the AR matrices exhibit only minor small-sample bias. Additionally, estimation accuracy appears to be slightly higher for the TV AR models compared to the LSTVAR models. While for most of the parameters there is only very slight bias, we find a notable upward small-sample bias for the scale parameter, for the degrees-of-freedom parameter whose true value is not small, and for the first entry in each impact matrix. The upward biases in the scale and not-small degrees-of-freedom parameters can both be explained by weak discrimination between large and very large estimates, as large values already imply nearly discrete

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5 Empirical application
texts.text: 5 Empirical application

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our empirical application studies the macroeconomic effects of climate policy uncertainty (CPU). In the related literature, Fried et al. (2022) develop a dynamic general equilibrium model incorporating beliefs about future climate policy, and find that an increased climate policy transition risk shifts investments toward cleaner capital while reducing total investments and output. Khalil and Strobel (2023) reach similar conclusions in their dynamic stochastic general equilibrium (DSGE) model, attributing the effects to financial institutions' aversion to uncertainty. Huang and Punzi (2024) also use a DSGE model and find that CPU shocks cause firms to delay investments, reducing output and increasing inflation, which is supported by results from their recursively identified Bayesian VAR.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We are interested in studying how the effects of the CPU shock vary depending on the level of general economic policy uncertainty (EPU). Such variation can be accommodated in our structural STVAR model by specifying a logistic transition weight function with the (lagged) level of EPU as the switching variable. This approach allows us to capture potential nonlinearities and regimedependent effects, providing more detailed results on the macroeconomic effects of the CPU shock than linear SVAR analysis.

texts.orig: 5.1 Data
texts.text: 5.1 Data

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We consider a monthly U.S. dataset consisting of six variables and covering the time period from 1987:4 to 2024:12. Following Khalil and Strobel (2023) and Huang and Punzi (2024), we measure CPU with the climate policy uncertainty index (CPUI) of Gavriilidis (2021), which is constructed based on the amount of newspaper coverage on climate policy uncertainty topics. The availability of the CPUI data determines the beginning of our sample period. In order to control for general economic policy uncertainty, we also include the economic policy uncertainty index (EPUI) (Baker et al. , 2016), which is based on newspaper coverage on topics related to economic policy uncertainty as well as on components quantifying the present value of future scheduled tax code expirations and disagreement among professional forecasters over future government purchases and consumer prices.

The skewed t -distribution can flexibly capture fat tails and skewness, and as the Gaussian distribution is obtained as a special case, with ν i = ∞ and λ i = 0 , the validity of the identifying Assumption 1 can be assessed based on the estimates of these parameters. The skewed t -distribution can flexibly capture fat tails and skewness, and as the Gaussian distribution is obtained as a special case, with ν i = ∞ and λ i = 0 , the validity of the identifying Assumption 1 can be assessed based on the estimates of these parameters. To avoid overfitting, particularly in the regime accommodating fewer observations, the autoregressive order p = 2 is selected based on Hannan-Quinn information criterion.

texts.orig: To deal with the problem of weak identification with respect to the ordering and signs of the columns of B 2 , discussed in Section 3.3, 3.4, and 3.5, we implement the following, simplistic blended identification strategy that largely avoids making restrictive assumptions about the effects of the CPU shock. We normalize the first row of B 1 positive with CPUI ordered first and assume that in both regimes, one of the shocks, deemed as the CPU shock, has greater impact effect on the CPUI than any other shock, and this impact effect has the same sign in both regimes. Then, to support our identification, we check that the recovered CPU shock aligns with several major historical events affecting CPU. In particular, we find that President Trump's election is preceded by large CPU shocks in October 2016 and 2024 ( 17 and 26 , respectively). The largest negative recovered CPU shock ( -10 ), in turn, dates to August 2022, when the Inflation Reduction Act was passed, providing support for the clean energy transition. Among all the shocks recovered from the model, only the one deemed as the CPU shock has signs consistent with the above historical events affecting CPU.

Therefore, we consider the general impulse response function (GIRF) (Koop, Pesaran, and Potter, 1996) that accommodates such features and is defined as

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: GIRF ( h, δ i , F t -1 ) = E [ y t + h | δ i , F t -1 ] -E [ y t + h |F t -1 ] , (5.1)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where h is the horizon. The first term on the right side of (5.1) is the expected realization of the process at time t + h conditionally on a structural shock of sign and size δ i ∈ R in the i th element of e t at time t and the previous observations. The latter term on the right side is the expected realization of the process conditionally on the previous observations only. The GIRF thus expresses the expected difference in the future outcomes when the i th structural shock of sign and size δ i arrives at time t as opposed to all shocks being random. The GIRFs also facilitate tracking the effects of the shock on the transition weights α m,t , m = 1 , ..., M , by replacing y t + h with α m,t + h on the right side of Equation (5.1).

texts.orig: In both regimes, a positive CPU shock increases consumer prices, in line with Huang and Punzi (2024) and Khalil and Strobel (2023). Huang and Punzi (2024) found that a positive CPU shock increases inflation by acting as a negative supply shock, whereas according to Khalil and Strobel (2023), a positive CPU shock may decrease aggregate demand due to risk-averse households raising savings, while firms raise their prices pre-emptively to lower the probability of being stuck with a negative markup. However, we find that the inflationary effects are stronger in the High EPU Regime than in the Low EPU Regime. One possible explanation is that the firms are more risk averse in the periods of high EPU, and thereby react more sensitively. Producer prices, in turn, mostly decrease in the Low EPU Regime but increase in the High EPU Regime.

texts.orig: 6 Conclusion
texts.text: 6 Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Linear structural vector autoregressive models can be identified statistically by non-Gaussianity without imposing restrictions on the model if the shocks are mutually independent and at most one of them is Gaussian (Lanne et al. , 2017). We have shown that this result extends to structural threshold and smooth transition vector autoregressive models incorporating a time-varying impact matrix defined as a weighted sum of the impact matrices of the regimes. The weights of the impact matrices are the transition weights of the regimes, which we assume to be either exogenous, logistic, or of the threshold form, but our results can be generalized to other suitable weight functions as well. Thus, the types of transition weights typically used in macroeconomic applications are accommodated.
texts.text: Linear structural vector autoregressive models can be identified statistically by non-Gaussianity without imposing restrictions on the model if the shocks are mutually independent and at most one of them is Gaussian (Lanne et al. , 2017). We have shown that this result extends to structural threshold and smooth transition vector autoregressive models incorporating a time-varying impact matrix defined as a weighted sum of the impact matrices of the regimes. The weights of the impact matrices are the transition weights of the regimes, which we assume to be either exogenous, logistic, or of the threshold form, but our results can be generalized to other suitable weight functions as well. Thus, the types of transition weights typically used in macroeconomic applications are accommodated.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In addition to establishing the identification of the shocks, we have discussed the problem of labelling them, and employed blended identification (cf. Carriero et al. , 2024) to address weak identification with respect to the ordering and signs of the columns of the regime-specific impact matrices. We have proposed estimating the model parameters by the method of penalized maximum likelihood, and introduced a three-step estimation procedure by adapting the approach of Lanne et al. (2017) to nonlinear SVAR models. Building on the results of Saikkonen (2008), we have also provided a sufficient condition for ergodic stationarity of our structural STV AR model. The introduced methods have been implemented in the accompanying R package sstvars (Virolainen, 2025a), which is available via the CRAN repository.

As a measure of climate policy uncertainty, we employed the CPU index (Gavriilidis, 2021) constructed based on the amount of newspaper coverage on climate policy uncertainty topics. To

texts.label: text
texts.orig: distinguish between periods low and high economic policy uncertainty, we used the first lag of the EPU index (Baker et al. , 2016) as the switching variable. Our results are in line with the previous literature suggesting that a positive CPU shock decreases production (Fried et al. , 2022, Khalil and Strobel, 2023, and Huang and Punzi, 2024) and increases inflation (Khalil and Strobel, 2023, and Huang and Punzi, 2024). However, we found that while a positive CPU shock decreases production and increases inflation in times of both low and high EPU, its inflationary effects are stronger during the periods of high EPU.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A Stationarity and ergodicity
texts.text: A Stationarity and ergodicity

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Establishing stationarity and ergodicity of the model is a common practice in time series econometrics, as it facilitates obtaining desirable asymptotic properties such as consistency of the estimator. Saikkonen (2008) derives a sufficient condition for ergodic stationarity of the smooth transition vector error correction (STVEC) model. Kheifets and Saikkonen (2020) make use of the results of Saikkonen (2008) to show that the stationarity condition readily applies for a STVAR model that can be obtained as a special case of the STVEC model of Saikkonen (2008). However, in both Saikkonen (2008) and Kheifets and Saikkonen (2020), the conditional covariance matrix of the process is defined as a weighted sum of positive definite error term covariance matrices of the regimes. As discussed in Section 3.1, the conditional covariance matrix (3.4) of our structural STVAR model has a different form, and therefore, our model is not nested to the model of Saikkonen (2008). The applicability of the results of Saikkonen (2008) also requires that the weights are not exogenous but functions of the preceding p observations (the time t weights are allowed to depend on the time t observation of an IID process independent of e t , but we do not discuss this option further, as it violates our assumption of F t -1 -measurable or nonrandom weights). Nonetheless, we will show in this section that the stationarity condition applies to our model as well.

However, in both Saikkonen (2008) and Kheifets and Saikkonen (2020), the conditional covariance matrix of the process is defined as a weighted sum of positive definite error term covariance matrices of the regimes. As discussed in Section 3.1, the conditional covariance matrix (3.4) of our structural STVAR model has a different form, and therefore, our model is not nested to the model of Saikkonen (2008). The applicability of the results of Saikkonen (2008) also requires that the weights are not exogenous but functions of the preceding p observations (the time t weights are allowed to depend on the time t observation of an IID process independent of e t , but we do not discuss this option further, as it violates our assumption of F t -1 -measurable or nonrandom weights). Nonetheless, we will show in this section that the stationarity condition applies to our model as well.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Condition (I) states that the JSR of the companion form AR matrices of the regimes is strictly less than one, and it is analogous to Condition (10) of Saikkonen (2008). Note that this condition is sufficient but not necessarily necessary for ergodic stationarity of the process (to the best of our knowledge, there does not currently exist results presenting a sufficient necessary condition for STVAR models). Condition (II) is analogous Assumption 1 of Saikkonen (2008), and it is innocuous in practice but rules out bounded error distributions, for example. Finally, Condition (III) ensures that the assumptions for the transition function imposed by Saikkonen (2008) are satisfied, ruling out nonrandom exogenous weights (which are accommodated by our identification results).

texts.orig: The following theorem, which is analogous to Theorem 1 in Saikkonen (2008) and Theorem 1 in Kheifets and Saikkonen (2020), states the results.
texts.text: The following theorem, which is analogous to Theorem 1 in Saikkonen (2008) and Theorem 1 in Kheifets and Saikkonen (2020), states the results.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem A.1. Consider the STVAR process y t defined in Equations (3.2) and (3.3), and suppose Assumptions 1 and A.1 are satisfied. Then, almost everywhere in [ B 1 : ... : B M ] ∈ R d × dM , the process y t = ( y t , ..., y t -p +1 ) is a (1 + || x || 2 ) -geometrically ergodic Markov chain. Thus, there exists a choice of initial values y -p +1 , ..., y 0 such that the process y t is strictly stationary, secondorder stationary, and β -mixing with geometrically decaying mixing numbers.
texts.text: Theorem A.1. Consider the STVAR process y t defined in Equations (3.2) and (3.3), and suppose Assumptions 1 and A.1 are satisfied. Then, almost everywhere in [ B 1 : ... : B M ] ∈ R d × dM , the process y t = ( y t , ..., y t -p +1 ) is a (1 + || x || 2 ) -geometrically ergodic Markov chain. Thus, there exists a choice of initial values y -p +1 , ..., y 0 such that the process y t is strictly stationary, secondorder stationary, and β -mixing with geometrically decaying mixing numbers.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem A.1 is proven in Appendix A.1. A notable difference of Theorem A.1 to Theorem 1 of Saikkonen (2008) (and Theorem 1 of Kheifets and Saikkonen, 2020) is that our results holds only almost everywhere in [ B 1 : ... : B M ] ∈ R d × dM , while the result of Saikkonen (2008) holds everywhere. To be more specific, the result of Theorem A.1 holds with such values of B 1 , ..., B M that the resulting impact matrix B y,t = ∑ M m =1 α m,t B m is invertible for all t , and Lemma 1 shows that this holds everywhere expect in regions of R d × dM that have a Lebesgue measure zero.

This makes it poorly suitable for restricting the parameter space in the numerical estimation of parameters discussed in Section 4. During estimation, this condition would have to be verified repeatedly a very large of times, making the estimation extremely tedious. Hence, it is useful make use of the following condition in the estimation, which is necessary for Assumption A.1(I).

texts.orig: Proof. If k = 1 , the proof is trivial. Suppose k > 1 . It then follows from Implicit function theorem that in the points x 0 = ( x 1 , 0 , ..., x k, 0 ) ∈ R k where the gradient of P ( x 1 , ..., x k ) is nonzero, the set X is an k -1 -dimensional manifold locally in a neighbourhood of x 0 . Because this argument applies at all points x 0 where the gradient of P ( x 1 , ..., x k ) is nonzero, it follows that the set X is an k -1 -dimensional manifold in regions where this condition holds. Therefore, Lebesque measure of the set X is zero in R k in the region where the gradient of P ( x 1 , ..., x k ) is nonzero. On the other hand, by Sard's theorem (Sard, 1994), the set of points where the gradient of P ( x 1 , ..., x k ) is zero has a Lebesgue measure zero in R k . texts.orig: To prove Lemma 1, we start by showing that for all α 1 ,t , ..., α M,t , the matrix B y,t = ∑ M m =1 α m,t B m is invertible almost everywhere in [ B 1 : ... : B M ] ∈ R d × dM . First, note that by the constraints ∑ m =1 α m,t = 1 and α m,t ≥ 0 , m = 1 , ..., M , we have α m,t > 0 for some m for all t . Therefore, for all α 1 ,t , ..., α M,t , there exists invertible matrices B 1 , ..., B M such that B y,t is invertible. The set of points [ B 1 : ... : B M ] ∈ R d × dM (or equally ( vec ( B 1 ) , ..., vec ( B M )) ∈ R Md 2 ) for which B y,t is singular is S t ≡ { ( vec ( B 1 ) , ..., vec ( B M )) ∈ R Md 2 | det( ∑ M m =1 α m,t B m ) = 0 } . Since the determinant of a matrix is polynomial in the entries of that matrix, and the entries of ∑ M m =1 α m,t B m are linear combinations of the entries of B 1 , ..., B M , it follows that the determinant of ∑ M m =1 α m,t B m is a polynomial in the entries of B 1 , ..., B M . Denote this polynomial as P t ( B 1 , ..., B M ) ≡ det( ∑ M m =1 α m,t B m ) . Note that whenever α m,t = 0 , the impact matrices B m of the corresponding regimes drop out from P t ( B 1 , ..., B M ) , but in this case P t ( B 1 , ..., B M ) can still be interpreted as a polynomial in the entries of B 1 , ..., B M with the multipliers of the dropped entries being zero. It then follows from Lemma B.1 that S t has a Lebesgue measure zero in R Md 2 .

texts.orig: Finally, note that since the above result holds for all α 1 ,t , ..., α M,t , the set S t has a Lebesgue measure zero in R Md 2 for each t . Hence, the union ∪ ∞ t =1 S t is a union of a countable number of measure zero sets and thus itself has a Lebesgue measure zero. It follows that the matrix B y,t is invertible for all t almost everywhere in [ B 1 : ... : B M ] ∈ R d × dM , concluding the proof.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We start by showing that under the conditions of Proposition 1, the ordering and signs of the columns of B y,t are fixed for all t in the sense that models before and after reordering or changing the signs of the columns of B y,t at any t are not observationally equivalent. Specifically, we say that two models are observational equivalent if and only if their joint densities are equal. After that, we combine this result with Lemma 2 to show that B 1 , ..., B M are uniquely identified.

texts.orig: Proof. Denote f ( x 1 , x 2 ) ≡ st ( x 1 ; ν i , λ i ) -st ( x 2 ; ν j , λ j ) , which is not identically zero by ( ν i , λ i ) = ( ν j , λ j ) . It is straightforward to show (by induction) that the function st ( x i ; ν i , λ i ) , and also thereby f ( x 1 , x 2 ) , is infinitely continuously differentiable. It then follows from Implicit function theorem that in the points x 0 = ( x 1 , 0 , x 2 , 0 ) ∈ R 2 where the gradient of f ( x 1 , x 2 ) is nonzero, the set X ≡ { ( x 1 , x 2 ) ∈ R 2 | f ( x 1 , x 2 ) = 0 } is a one-dimensional manifold locally in a neighbourhood of x 0 . Because this argument applies at all points x 0 where the gradient of f ( x 1 , x 2 ) is nonzero, it follows that the set X is a one-dimensional manifold in regions where this condition holds. Therefore, Lebesque measure of the set X is zero in R 2 in the region where the gradient of f ( x 1 , x 2 ) is nonzero. On the other hand, by Sard's theorem (Sard, 1994), the set of points where the gradient of f ( x 1 , x 2 ) is zero has a Lebesgue measure zero in R 2 . Since the union of two sets with a Lebesgue measure zero has a Lebesgue measure zero, these results can be combined to conclude that the X has a Lebesgue measure zero in R 2 . That is, st ( x 1 ; ν i , λ i ) = st ( x 2 ; ν j , λ j ) only on sets of zero Lebesgue measure, concluding that st ( x 1 ; ν i , λ i ) = st ( x 2 ; ν j , λ j ) almost everywhere in ( x 1 , x 2 ) ∈ R 2 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The proof consists of two parts. First, we show that if the ordering and signs of the columns of B 1 is fixed, changing the ordering or signs of the columns of any of B 2 , ..., B M leads to an impact matrix B y,t that cannot, at all t , be obtained by reordering or changing the signs of the columns of the original impact matrix. In other words, we show that fixing the ordering signs of the columns of B 1 effectively fixes the ordering and signs of the columns of B 2 , ..., B M and thereby of B y,t . Then, we make use of this result together with the result of Lemma 2 (that B y,t is identified up ordering and signs of its columns at each t ) to show that the matrices B 1 , ..., B M are uniquely identified. For models with logistic transition weights (2.4), the result holds almost everywhere in [ B 1 : · · · : B M ] ∈ R d × dM , ( y -p +1 , ..., y 0 , y 1 , ..., y T -1 ) ∈ R d ( T + p -1) and, if the switching variable z t is exogenous, ( z 1 , ..., z T ) ∈ R T . For models with nonrandom exogenous transition weights, the result holds almost everywhere in [ B 1 : · · · : B M ] ∈ R d × dM .

Since the ordering and signs of the columns of B 1 are fixed (Condition (A) of Proposition 2), we then obtain from the above results that almost everywhere in [ B 1 : ... : B M ] ∈ R d × dM , reordering or changing the signs of the columns of B 2 , ..., B M would, at some t , lead to an impact matrix that cannot obtained by reordering or changing the signs of the columns of B y,t . texts.label: text
texts.orig: Saikkonen (2008) establishes stationarity, ergodicity, and mixing properties of a STVEC model by making use of the theory of Markov chains. Kheifets and Saikkonen (2020), in turn, show that the results of Saikkonen (2008) are applicable to their STVAR model, as it can be obtained as a special case of the STVEC model of Saikkonen (2008). However, the results of Saikkonen (2008) and Kheifets and Saikkonen (2020) are not directly applicable to our structural STVAR model, because our model defines the conditional covariance matrix differently to the STVEC model of Saikkonen (2008) and STVAR model of Kheifets and Saikkonen (2020). Hence, certain steps need to be taken show the applicability of the results of Saikkonen (2008) to our structural STVAR model.

After describing the NLS estimation, penalized nonlinear least squares (PNLS) estimation is considered by introducing a penalization term to the sum of squares of residuals function.

texts.orig: First, determine the values of the transition weights α m,t , m = 1 , ..., M , t = 1 , ..., T , that can be deemed reasonable for estimation, i.e., transition weights that allocate a sufficient enough contribution to each regime in the model. As an extreme example, transition weights with α 1 ,t = 0 for all t = 1 , ..., T are not reasonable, as Regime 1 would not be involved in the model at all. Thus, each regime should contribute to the model to an extend that is reasonable enough for estimation.

Thus, each regime should contribute to the model to an extend that is reasonable enough for estimation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: One way to assess the contribution of each regime is by summing over the transition weights, i.e., calculating the sums ˙ α m ≡ ∑ T t =1 α m,t , m = 1 , ..., M . In the special case of discrete transition weights such as the threshold weights (2.3), the sums ˙ α m express the numbers of observations from each regime. Therefore, one way to determine whether a regime contributes enough to the model is to check whether the sums ˙ α m are large enough compared to the number of parameters in that regime for all m = 1 , ..., M . Denote the large enough sum as ˜ T . We assume that if there are k parameters in a regime, and there should be at least three times as many observations from all the d variables combined, implying ˜ T = 3 kd -1 , then transition weights that satisfy ˙ α m ≥ ˜ T for all m = 1 , ..., M are deemed reasonable enough. 6
texts.text: One way to assess the contribution of each regime is by summing over the transition weights, i.e., calculating the sums ˙ α m ≡ ∑ T t =1 α m,t , m = 1 , ..., M . In the special case of discrete transition weights such as the threshold weights (2.3), the sums ˙ α m express the numbers of observations from each regime. Therefore, one way to determine whether a regime contributes enough to the model is to check whether the sums ˙ α m are large enough compared to the number of parameters in that regime for all m = 1 , ..., M . Denote the large enough sum as ˜ T . We assume that if there are k parameters in a regime, and there should be at least three times as many observations from all the d variables combined, implying ˜ T = 3 kd -1 , then transition weights that satisfy ˙ α m ≥ ˜ T for all m = 1 , ..., M are deemed reasonable enough. 6

texts.orig: The results of the Monte Carlo study are presented in Table D.2 for the TVAR models and in Table D.3 for the LSTVAR models. The results show that there is some but mostly very small bias, which diminishes when the sample size increases. Since also the standard deviation of the estimates diminishes with the sample size, our results are in line with consistency of the PML estimator. Nonetheless, for certain parameters, there appears to be a notable bias in small samples.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: First, the estimator of the second degrees-of-freedom parameter (true parameter value ν 2 = 12 ) is substantially biased upwards in samples of length T = 250 , 500 , and 1000 , and also the standard deviation of the estimates is very large. This can explained by the property of the skewed t -distribution that when the degrees-of-freedom parameter value is already large, increasing it more has virtually no effect on the shape of the distribution. Consequently, if a large degrees-of-freedom parameter fits the data, the numerical optimization algorithms are incapable of discriminating between large and very large parameter values. Hence, possibly very large degrees-of-freedom parameter values drawn by random in Step 2 estimation may remain large through Step 3 estimation. Since it is clear that there is virtually no difference in the economic interpretations of large (say, 100 ) and very large (say, 1000 ) degrees-of-freedom parameter values, the large upward bias should not be a concern.

The differences are, however, only small, and even in small samples, there does not appear to be any particular issues with the estimation accuracy of the AR matrices lying inside the penalization region. Also, the TVAR models seem to be slightly more accurately estimated than the LSTVAR models, possibly because, unlike in TVAR models, in LSTVAR models also the smoothness of regime-switches needs to be estimated.

texts.orig: To study whether our model adequately captures the autocorrelation structure of the data, we depict the sample autocorrelation functions (ACF) and cross-correlation functions (CCF) of the (nonstandardized) residuals for the first 24 lags in Figure E.1. There is not much autocorrelation left in the residuals, but there is a particularly large correlation coefficient (CC) in the lag zero CCFs between the fourth and fifth residual series. In addition, there are a few moderately sized CCs in the ACFs and CCFs of some of the residual series, but the vast majority of the CCs are small. Hence, with the exception of the single large CC in the lag zero CCFs of the fourth and fifth residual series, our LSTVAR model appears to capture the autocorrelation structure of the data reasonably well.

texts.orig: To study our model's adequacy to capture the conditional heteroscedasticity in the data, we depict the sample ACFs and CCFs of squared standardized residuals for the first 24 lags in Figure E.2. There is a very large CC in the lag zero CCFs between the second and third residual series, which, however, disappears if the COVID-19 residual from March 2020 is excluded (not shown). In addition, there are several sizeable CCs in the ACFs and CCFs of some of the residual series. Thus, there are some inadequacies in capturing the conditional heteroscedasticity, but with the exception of the large CC in CCFs between the second and third residual series (due to the extreme COVID-19 volatility), the inadequacies do not seem particularly severe.
